metadata.summary: Assign RACI (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) roles to tasks. Exactly one Accountable per task; cap Consulted at 3-4 to avoid bottlenecks.

content.title: RACI Matrix
content.summary: Assign one of four roles — Responsible (does the work), Accountable (single decision owner), Consulted (provides input), Informed (notified after) — to each role for each task or deliverable. The rule: exactly one Accountable per task, no exceptions. Multiple Accountables collapse the escalation path and hide conflicts that only sponsors can resolve.

why.p: "I thought you were handling that" and "who approves this?" are symptoms of undefined accountability. RACI makes implicit role assumptions explicit before work begins, so disputes surface in a kickoff meeting rather than at a missed deadline. The Consulted count is a bottleneck detector: more than 3-4 Consulted per task signals that decisions will be slow.

list.item: New project kickoff with multiple roles (PM, dev lead, QA, DevOps, BA, sponsor) and recurring "who decides?" friction.
Cross-team features where SDD task ownership is ambiguous (backend + frontend + data + ops).
Vendor/contractor engagements: clarify what client owns vs what contractor delivers.
Audit/compliance projects (SOC2, ISO 27001) requiring a named Accountable per control.
Solopreneur engagements with designer, developer, and VA mix.
One-person solo task with no external stakeholders — overhead with zero return.
Pure agile teams with collective code ownership and one PO — RACI flattens to "PO=A, team=R" everywhere; use DACI for decisions instead.
Highly emergent work where roles shift weekly — matrix decays faster than it is updated.

section.name: role-definitions
section.p: RACI defines four roles per task. Exactly one role must be Accountable; multiple Responsible is acceptable; Consulted must be capped; Informed should be broad.

list.item: R — Responsible: does the work; can be multiple people per task.
A — Accountable: single final decision maker; escalation endpoint; only ONE per task.
C — Consulted: provides input before decision; two-way communication; keep to minimum.
I — Informed: notified after decision; one-way communication; be generous with I.

rule.statement: Every task must have exactly one Accountable — no shared A, no A/A splits.
rule.rationale: Two Accountables means neither can make a final decision unilaterally. Teams compromise on "A1/A2", which hides the escalation path and requires a third party to break ties at the worst possible moment.

rule.statement: Every task must have at least one Responsible.
rule.rationale: A task with only A, C, or I has no one committed to doing the actual work. It will be ignored until the Accountable notices it has not been done.

rule.statement: Cap Consulted at 3-4 per task; more than 4 is a bottleneck signal.
rule.rationale: Each Consulted requires a round-trip communication before work proceeds. Beyond 4, the coordination overhead exceeds the value of the input received.

section.name: construction-steps
section.p: Build the RACI matrix in five steps, starting with work breakdown and ending with a kickoff walkthrough.

list.item: Step 1 — List tasks: use WBS work packages or deliverables as rows.
Step 2 — List roles: use role titles, not individual names — roles survive personnel changes.
Step 3 — Assign RACI values: start with A (who owns the outcome?), then R (who does the work?), then C and I.
Step 4 — Validate: check all four rules; do NOT auto-resolve violations — flag for human.
Step 5 — Walk in kickoff: review cell-by-cell with all roles present; silent acknowledgement is not consent.

rule.statement: Walk the RACI in the kickoff meeting cell-by-cell with all roles present.
rule.rationale: Paper sign-off on a matrix nobody read produces false consensus. Verbal walkthrough surfaces "I didn't know I was Accountable for that" before work begins, not at the deadline.

section.name: validation-rules
section.p: After initial assignment, validate the matrix against these four rules. Do not silently auto-correct; flag violations to the Accountable for resolution.

list.item: No A: assign an Accountable or escalate to sponsor to pick one.
Multiple A: force a single choice; surface the conflict to the sponsor if roles dispute ownership.
No R: assign a Responsible party or remove the task from scope.
All I in a row: the role is not needed for this task — remove from matrix.
C count > 4: reduce to essential Consulted only; convert remainder to I.

example.title: Feature Development Team RACI
example.code: Task | PM | Product | Engineering | Design | QA --------------------|----|---------|--------------|---------|----| Define requirements | C | A | C | C | I Create mockups | I | C | I | R | I Approve design | I | A | C | R | I Write code | I | C | R | I | I Code review | I | I | A | I | I Write tests | I | I | C | I | R Deploy to staging | C | I | R | I | C UAT sign-off | I | A | I | I | R Production deploy | A | I | R | I | C
example.note: Engineering is A for Code Review (code quality) but R for Write Code — Accountable and Responsible are separated to preserve escalation paths.

example.title: Solopreneur with Contractors RACI
example.code: Task | You | Designer | Developer | VA ------------|------|----------|-----------|---- Vision | A/R | I | I | I UI design | A | R | C | I Development | A | C | R | I Testing | A | I | C | R Launch | A/R | I | I | C
example.note: Even with small teams, RACI prevents "I thought you were handling that" disputes with contractors who have parallel commitments.

antipattern.description: A=R for the senior role on every task.
antipattern.reason: LLMs and inexperienced PMs assign A=R to the project lead because charters say "PM owns delivery". The Accountable should not be doing the work — that conflation breaks the escalation chain.

antipattern.description: Assigning C to every named role "to be thorough".
antipattern.reason: Every C requires a feedback round-trip. A matrix with 8 Consulted per task is a meeting schedule, not a responsibility model. Set an explicit cap (3-4 C per task) in the generation prompt.

antipattern.description: Storing RACI only in Confluence with no version history.
antipattern.reason: Role changes are invisible without diffs. Keep RACI in source control as CSV; generate Markdown view for Confluence. Agents should output a unified diff when updating, not a full rewrite.

antipattern.description: Agent silently fixes violations by reassigning roles without human review.
antipattern.reason: Every role change has organizational implications. Violations must surface to the human Accountable for resolution — the agent flags but never resolves autonomously.

section.name: agent-integration-guidelines
section.p: When using agents to generate or validate RACI matrices, follow these practices to ensure the output aligns with organizational reality.

rule.statement: Force-feed the cap "Consulted ≤ 4 per task" into every agent prompt that generates RACI assignments.
rule.rationale: Agents default to including every stakeholder as Consulted because it seems inclusive. Without explicit constraint, every matrix balloons to 8+ Consulted per task, rendering the matrix unusable.

rule.statement: Require agents to output a violation report (using raci-validate.py) before finalizing any matrix.
rule.rationale: Agents miss edge cases like "all-I rows" and "A=R antipatterns" if you do not force a structured validation step. The script surfaces violations; the human decides resolution.

rule.statement: When updating an existing RACI, output a unified diff (CSV row-by-row) showing what changed, not a full rewrite.
rule.rationale: Full rewrites hide which roles changed, making it hard to discuss impacts. Diffs make role changes explicit and traceable.

list.item: Input: WBS (list of tasks), list of roles, candidate RACI assignments from domain knowledge or user sketch.
Generation: fill matrix with RACI values, capping C ≤ 4 per task.
Validation: run raci-validate.py; emit violation report if any rule breaks.
Review: human Accountables sign off on assignments; agents flag but do not auto-correct.
Output: CSV with version date; Markdown render for Confluence; diff if updating existing matrix.
Use role titles, not names. "Product Manager", not "Alice Smith" — roles survive people.
Separate A and R when possible. The Accountable owns the decision; the Responsible executes it. Conflating both breaks escalation.
Keep the matrix alive. Reassign when roles change; version it in source control. Stale matrices breed confusion.
Walk in kickoff. Silent acknowledgement of a matrix is not consent. Present cell-by-cell; ask "does this match your understanding?"
Escalate violations to the sponsor, not to the agent. Multiple A or missing A means organizational ambiguity — humans decide the tie-break.
Use diffs, not rewrites. When updating, emit what changed so teams can discuss impacts.

template.purpose: Blank RACI grid template with role columns and task rows

script.purpose: CSV validator checking all four rules with per-violation error output
